prompt:
Use case: stylized-concept
Asset type: project-bound RPG 1920s cosmic-horror atlas map, first batch pass, landscape orientation
Primary request: Call of Cthulhu / 1920s Lovecraftian inspired world map, continent plus archipelago terrain type.
Scene/backdrop: overhead birds-eye aged parchment atlas view of one dominant fog-shrouded continent with a large uneven offshore archipelago.
Subject: one main continent with complex New England-style coastlines, misty gulfs, bays, capes, narrow straits, broken rocky shores, marshy river deltas, inland lakes, and many irregular island chains offshore. Islands must be unevenly distributed with varied sizes and shapes: lighthouse islands, tiny haunted islets, craggy islands, reef rings, lagoons, narrow channels, remote atolls, drowned temple islands, cult hideouts, abandoned fishing villages, and impossible monolith islands. Include Arkham-like towns, Miskatonic river settlements, old colonial ports, foggy hills, standing stones, graveyards, forests, wetlands, tundra, mountains, and bleak coastal regions.
Style/medium: 1920s New England town map expanded to continent scale, Lovecraftian horror, aged parchment with hand annotations but no readable text, occult cartography, highly detailed illustrated RPG sourcebook map.
Composition/framing: wide 16:9 landscape, dominant continent offset to one side and archipelago sweeping diagonally around it, no simple grid spacing, full map visible.
Lighting/mood: creeping cosmic dread, Jazz Age antiquarian mystery, fog, impossible shadows, ancient gods beneath the sea, forbidden routes, sanity-eroding atmosphere.
Color palette: aged sepia parchment, cold grey-blue ocean, faded ink, sickly green occult accents, fog grey, dark pine forests, autumn browns.
Materials/textures: brittle paper, ink wash, etched coastlines, faint coffee stains, marginal occult sigils without readable lettering.
Constraints: no text labels, no letters, no readable words, no legend, no compass text, no watermark, no UI, no characters or portraits. Islands should feel natural and irregular, not evenly spaced.
